title: [Makefile] Don't compile during install
body:
Usually while building from source, `make` and `make install` is a common phenomenon. Former only compiles and latter only installs.

Standard procedure of `make && make install` compiles same package twice wasting CPU cycles and producing heat which causes climate change. So trying to fix that. #MakeEarthGreenAgain

comments.body: Ephemeral COPR build failed. @containers/packit-build please check.

while I support and prefer install targets not building, this will break users and scripts expecting everything to work with just `make install`. 

@mtrmac looks like podman and buildah have switched to not building in install targets. Would you be cool with making the switch here too? If we don't want to break anything, we could have `install.nobuild` targets and then have the install target depend on `install.nobuild + build` targets?

> I think, as a general principle, computers should work to remove tedious work. 

I don't think just typing extra `make` prior to `make install` is tedious work. 

 > And then `install` can depend on the binary artifact for users convenience, while the usual `make && {,sudo} make install` users will only trigger one compilation.

*recompilation

User already sacrifices own "User convenience" when one decides to compile from source instead of using package manager :) 

Restricting options and diverting from standards in the name of avg joe's convenience is Apple way. Personally, I hate apples, love open raspberries.

> > I think, as a general principle, computers should work to remove tedious work.
> 
> I don't think just typing extra `make` prior to `make install` is tedious work.

I’m open to that argument, given GNU coding standards. Typing extra `make docs` or `make completions`, _and having to figure that out_, does count as a tedious work to me. Nobody wakes up with “I want to run `make completions` today”.

> > And then `install` can depend on the binary artifact for users convenience, while the usual `make && {,sudo} make install` users will only trigger one compilation.
> 
> *recompilation

No, my point is that with properly specified dependencies `make install` _does the right thing_: it compiles the binary if the user didn’t run `make`, and it doesn’t compile again if `make` was run without modifying the sources.

> it compiles the binary if the user didn’t run `make`, and it doesn’t compile again if `make` was run without modifying the sources.

But it still checks if it was previously compiled or not. This step is considered as "building". The reason I insist that `make install` should _JUST_ install (nothing else) is most package managers consider **building** in **install** phase as a red flag and not best practice.

I don’t know how to agree on what is a “best practice”; opinions will vary.

Pragmatically, any Linux package manager has certainly had to install with GNU coding standards-targeting software; so it either has a known technique to reconcile these two approaches, or a long-standing practice of ignoring the difference between them.

> I don’t know how to agree on what is a “best practice”; opinions will vary.
> 
> Pragmatically, any Linux package manager has certainly had to install with GNU coding standards-targeting software; so it either has a known technique to reconcile these two approaches, or a long-standing practice of ignoring the difference between them.

I respect your decision. Will close the PR.
@lsm5 Would it be valuable to add the precise dependencies, so that a second `make` doesn’t recompile? Or would you be patching that code out either way?
